does: This paper introduces the Precise Debugging Benchmark (PDB), a framework that systematically evaluates whether large language models can perform precise bug fixing rather than simply regenerating correct code. The framework automatically converts coding datasets into debugging benchmarks by synthesizing verified atomic bugs and composing them into multi-bug programs. The authors define two key metrics: edit-level precision, measuring the proportion of necessary edits made without superfluous changes, and bug-level recall, measuring the fraction of bugs successfully resolved. They release two benchmarks (PDB-Single-Hard for single-line bugs and PDB-Multi for multi-line bugs) and demonstrate through experiments that frontier models like GPT-4 and DeepSeek-V3 achieve high unit-test pass rates above 76% but exhibit precision below 45%, revealing a significant gap between generating correct solutions and making minimal, targeted fixes.
connects: This work builds on prior research in code generation and program repair that has primarily focused on whether models can produce correct code, typically evaluated through test case pass rates. The paper responds to limitations in existing benchmarks like HumanEval and CodeBench, which measure functional correctness but cannot distinguish between precise repairs and wholesale code regeneration. It extends recent work on code editing and retrieval-augmented generation for debugging by introducing a more nuanced evaluation paradigm. The research connects to broader lines of work on neural program synthesis, automated debugging, and the evaluation methodology for code LLMs, acknowledging that functional correctness alone is insufficient for understanding model capabilities in realistic software engineering workflows.
enables: This framework opens avenues for developing LLMs that perform surgical, minimal edits during debugging rather than broad regeneration, which is critical for real-world code maintenance scenarios where developers need to understand and trust model changes. The precision-aware metrics enable more granular analysis of where models fail—whether in fault localization, patch generation, or both. Future work can leverage PDB to study how architectural choices, training procedures, and prompting strategies affect debugging precision, potentially leading to specialized fine-tuning approaches or new training objectives that penalize unnecessary edits. The benchmark also supports research into iterative debugging agents that maintain code context and progressively fix bugs with minimal modification, as well as work on interpretability and explainability in code repair systems where users need to understand why and where code changed.
